question: What is the capital of France?
note: Paris has been France's capital for over 1,000 years.

question: What is the capital of Australia?
note: Canberra became Australia's capital in 1913 as a compromise between Sydney and Melbourne.

question: What is the capital of Brazil?
note: Brasília replaced Rio de Janeiro as Brazil's capital in 1960.

question: What is the capital of Canada?
note: Ottawa has been Canada's capital since 1857, chosen by Queen Victoria.

question: What is the capital of Japan?
note: Tokyo has been Japan's capital since 1869, when Emperor Meiji moved there.

question: What is the capital of Germany?
note: Berlin became the unified Germany's capital again in 1990 after reunification.

question: What is the capital of Argentina?
note: Buenos Aires means 'Fair Winds' in Spanish — founded in 1536.

question: What is the capital of South Africa?
note: Pretoria is the executive capital. South Africa has three capitals — also Cape Town (legislative) and Bloemfontein (judicial).

question: What is the capital of Egypt?
note: Cairo is Africa's largest city with over 20 million people in its metro area.

question: What is the capital of Mexico?
note: Mexico City is one of the world's largest cities, built on the ruins of Tenochtitlan.

question: What is the capital of India?
note: New Delhi was designed by British architect Edwin Lutyens and became capital in 1931.

question: What is the capital of China?
note: Beijing has served as China's capital for most of the last 700 years.

question: What is the capital of Russia?
note: Moscow has been Russia's capital since 1918, and before that from 1328 to 1712.

question: What is the capital of Nigeria?
note: Abuja replaced Lagos as Nigeria's capital in 1991, chosen for its central location.

question: What is the capital of South Korea?
note: Seoul is home to about half of South Korea's total population.

question: What is the capital of Turkey?
note: Ankara became Turkey's capital in 1923 when Atatürk founded the modern republic.

question: What is the capital of Spain?
note: Madrid became Spain's capital in 1561 under King Philip II.

question: What is the capital of Italy?
note: Rome has been Italy's capital since 1871 and was the centre of the Roman Empire.

question: What is the capital of Poland?
note: Warsaw is also known as the 'Phoenix City' — rebuilt after being almost completely destroyed in WWII.

question: What is the capital of Saudi Arabia?
note: Riyadh means 'The Gardens' in Arabic — it sits in the heart of the Arabian Peninsula.

question: What is the capital of Iran?
note: Tehran became Iran's capital in 1796 under the Qajar dynasty.

question: What is the capital of Indonesia?
note: Indonesia is actually moving its capital to Nusantara on Borneo, expected in 2045.

question: What is the capital of Pakistan?
note: Islamabad replaced Karachi as Pakistan's capital in 1966 — purpose-built from scratch.

question: What is the capital of Ukraine?
note: Kyiv is one of Europe's oldest cities, founded in the 5th century AD.

question: What is the capital of Kenya?
note: Nairobi was founded in 1899 as a railway camp during the construction of the Uganda Railway.

question: What is the capital of Ethiopia?
note: Addis Ababa means 'New Flower' in Amharic and is the seat of the African Union.

question: What is the capital of Morocco?
note: Rabat has been Morocco's capital since 1912 — Casablanca is the largest city but not the capital.

question: What is the capital of Thailand?
note: Bangkok's full ceremonial name is the longest place name in the world at 168 characters.

question: What is the capital of Vietnam?
note: Hanoi has been Vietnam's capital since 1010, though Ho Chi Minh City is larger.

question: What is the capital of Sweden?
note: Stockholm was founded in 1252 and is built across 14 islands.

question: What is the capital of Norway?
note: Oslo has been Norway's capital since 1814 and was called Christiania from 1624 to 1925.

question: What is the capital of Finland?
note: Helsinki became Finland's capital in 1812, replacing Turku at Russia's request.

question: What is the capital of Denmark?
note: Copenhagen has been Denmark's capital since the early 15th century.

question: What is the capital of Netherlands?
note: Amsterdam is the constitutional capital but The Hague is the seat of government.

question: What is the capital of Belgium?
note: Brussels is also the de facto capital of the European Union.

question: What is the capital of Switzerland?
note: Bern is Switzerland's federal city — Switzerland technically has no official 'capital'.

question: What is the capital of Austria?
note: Vienna was the capital of the Habsburg Empire for 600 years.

question: What is the capital of Portugal?
note: Lisbon is one of Europe's oldest cities and the westernmost capital of continental Europe.

question: What is the capital of Greece?
note: Athens has been continuously inhabited for over 3,000 years.

question: What is the capital of Romania?
note: Bucharest is known as the 'Little Paris of the East' for its Belle Époque architecture.

question: What is the capital of Hungary?
note: Budapest was formed in 1873 by merging Buda, Óbuda, and Pest across the Danube.

question: What is the capital of Czech Republic?
note: Prague Castle is the largest ancient castle complex in the world.

question: What is the capital of Kazakhstan?
note: Astana (formerly Nur-Sultan) replaced Almaty as Kazakhstan's capital in 1997.

question: What is the capital of New Zealand?
note: Wellington has been New Zealand's capital since 1865 — Auckland is the largest city.

question: What is the capital of Peru?
note: Lima was founded by Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro in 1535.

question: What is the capital of Colombia?
note: Bogotá sits at 2,600m above sea level — one of the world's highest capital cities.

question: What is the capital of Chile?
note: Santiago is home to about 40% of Chile's entire population.

question: What is the capital of Philippines?
note: Manila is one of the most densely populated cities on Earth.

question: What is the capital of Malaysia?
note: Kuala Lumpur means 'Muddy Confluence' in Malay — it grew from a tin-mining settlement.

question: What is the capital of Singapore?
note: Singapore is a city-state — the entire country is essentially one city.

question: What is the capital of Bangladesh?
note: Dhaka is one of the world's most densely populated cities with over 22 million people.

question: What is the capital of Sri Lanka?
note: Sri Jayawardenepura Kotte is the legislative capital; Colombo is the commercial capital.

question: What is the capital of Myanmar?
note: Naypyidaw replaced Yangon as Myanmar's capital in 2006 in a surprise government move.

question: What is the capital of Ghana?
note: Accra became Ghana's capital under British colonial rule and remained so after independence in 1957.

question: What is the capital of Tanzania?
note: Dodoma is Tanzania's official capital since 1996, but Dar es Salaam remains the largest city.

question: What is the capital of Zimbabwe?
note: Harare was called Salisbury during British colonial rule, renamed in 1982.

question: What is the capital of Senegal?
note: Dakar is the westernmost point of continental Africa.

question: What is the capital of Ivory Coast?
note: Yamoussoukro is the official capital but Abidjan remains the economic and administrative centre.

question: What is the capital of Cuba?
note: Havana was founded in 1519 and is famous for its preserved colonial architecture.

question: What is the capital of Jamaica?
note: Kingston has been Jamaica's capital since 1872.

question: What is the capital of Iceland?
note: Reykjavík is the world's northernmost national capital.

question: What is the capital of Ireland?
note: Dublin was founded by Vikings in 841 AD.

question: What is the capital of Croatia?
note: Zagreb has been Croatia's capital since 1776.

question: What is the capital of Serbia?
note: Belgrade means 'White City' in Serbian and sits at the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers.

question: What is the capital of Slovakia?
note: Bratislava sits on the Danube, bordering both Austria and Hungary.

question: What is the capital of Slovenia?
note: Ljubljana's name may derive from a Slavic root meaning 'beloved'.

question: What is the capital of Estonia?
note: Tallinn's medieval old town is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: What is the capital of Latvia?
note: Riga is the largest city in the Baltic states.

question: What is the capital of Lithuania?
note: Vilnius has one of the largest medieval old towns in Northern Europe.

question: What is the capital of Belarus?
note: Minsk was almost completely destroyed in WWII and rebuilt in Stalinist architectural style.

question: What is the capital of Moldova?
note: Chișinău is one of the greenest capitals in Europe, with many parks and tree-lined streets.

question: What is the capital of Georgia (country)?
note: Tbilisi was founded in the 5th century and its name means 'warm location'.

question: What is the capital of Armenia?
note: Yerevan is one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, founded in 782 BC.

question: What is the capital of Azerbaijan?
note: Baku sits below sea level on the Caspian Sea and is a major oil-producing city.

question: What is the capital of Uzbekistan?
note: Tashkent became the USSR's fourth largest city and was almost destroyed by an earthquake in 1966.

question: What is the capital of Nepal?
note: Kathmandu Valley contains seven UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

question: What is the capital of Afghanistan?
note: Kabul is one of the world's fastest-growing cities and sits at 1,800m altitude.

question: What is the capital of Iraq?
note: Baghdad was founded in 762 AD and was once the world's largest city.

question: What is the capital of Syria?
note: Damascus is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.

question: What is the capital of Jordan?
note: Amman is built on seven hills — the same number as Rome — and was called Philadelphia in antiquity.

question: What is the capital of Lebanon?
note: Beirut was known as the 'Paris of the Middle East' before its civil war.

question: What is the capital of Israel?
note: Jerusalem is claimed as capital by Israel; it is not universally recognised internationally.

question: What is the capital of Oman?
note: Muscat sits between the Al Hajar mountains and the Gulf of Oman.

question: What is the capital of Kuwait?
note: Kuwait City contains most of Kuwait's population and virtually all its government institutions.

question: What is the capital of Qatar?
note: Doha hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup — the first in the Middle East.

question: What is the capital of UAE?
note: Abu Dhabi is the UAE's capital — Dubai is the largest city and financial hub.

question: What is the capital of Bahrain?
note: Manama has been Bahrain's capital since 1971 when the country gained independence.

question: What is the capital of Yemen?
note: Sana'a's old city is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the world's oldest cities.

question: What is the capital of Cameroon?
note: Yaoundé is Cameroon's capital while Douala is its economic heart.

question: What is the capital of Angola?
note: Luanda is one of Africa's most expensive cities, largely due to the oil industry.

question: What is the capital of Mozambique?
note: Maputo was called Lourenço Marques under Portuguese rule until 1976.

question: What is the capital of Madagascar?
note: Antananarivo means 'City of Thousands' and sits at 1,400m altitude.

question: What is the capital of Tunisia?
note: Tunis sits next to the ruins of ancient Carthage, once Rome's greatest rival.

question: What is the capital of Algeria?
note: Algiers is known as 'Alger la Blanche' — White Algiers — for its whitewashed buildings.

question: What is the capital of Libya?
note: Tripoli has been Libya's capital since the Ottoman period.

question: What is the capital of Sudan?
note: Khartoum sits at the confluence of the Blue and White Nile rivers.

question: What is the capital of Uganda?
note: Kampala is built on seven hills and was the seat of the Buganda Kingdom.

question: What is the capital of Rwanda?
note: Kigali is consistently ranked as one of Africa's cleanest cities.

question: What is the capital of Zambia?
note: Lusaka became Zambia's capital when the country gained independence in 1964.

question: What is the capital of Botswana?
note: Gaborone is one of the world's fastest-growing cities and sits near the South Africa border.

question: What currency is used in Japan?
note: The Japanese Yen (¥) has been in use since 1871.

question: What currency is used in the United Kingdom?
note: The British pound is the world's oldest currency still in use, over 1,200 years old.

question: What currency is used in Switzerland?
note: Switzerland never adopted the Euro despite being surrounded by Eurozone countries.

question: What currency is used in South Korea?
note: The South Korean Won (KRW) has been used since 1945.

question: What currency is used in Brazil?
note: The Brazilian Real replaced the cruzeiro in 1994 as part of an anti-inflation plan.

question: What currency is used in India?
note: The Indian Rupee (₹) symbol was officially adopted in 2010.

question: What currency is used in Russia?
note: The Russian Ruble is one of the world's oldest currencies, dating to the 13th century.

question: What currency is used in China?
note: Renminbi means 'People's Currency' — Yuan is the main unit. Symbol: ¥ or 元.

question: What currency is used in Saudi Arabia?
note: The Saudi Riyal has been pegged to the US Dollar since 1986.

question: What currency is used in Turkey?
note: The Turkish Lira replaced the Ottoman Lira in 1923. The 'new' Lira was introduced in 2005.

question: What currency is used in Poland?
note: The Polish Złoty (PLN) has been Poland's currency since 1924.

question: What currency is used in Mexico?
note: The Mexican Peso is one of the first currencies in the world to use the $ symbol.

question: What currency is used in Norway?
note: The Norwegian Krone (NOK) — Norway chose not to join the Eurozone.

question: What currency is used in Sweden?
note: Sweden uses the Krona (SEK). In a 2003 referendum, Swedes voted against joining the Eurozone.

question: What currency is used in Denmark?
note: The Danish Krone (DKK) — Denmark has an opt-out from the Euro.

question: What currency is used in Egypt?
note: The Egyptian Pound dates back to 1834 and is subdivided into 100 piasters.

question: What currency is used in Nigeria?
note: The Nigerian Naira replaced the Pound in 1973 after the country decimalised.

question: What currency is used in Kenya?
note: Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania all use currencies called Shillings, though they're separate.

question: What currency is used in South Africa?
note: The South African Rand (ZAR) is named after the Witwatersrand gold-bearing reef.

question: What currency is used in Morocco?
note: The Moroccan Dirham (MAD) replaced the Franc in 1960.

question: What currency is used in Indonesia?
note: Indonesia's Rupiah was introduced in 1946 — the word derives from Sanskrit for 'silver'.

question: What currency is used in Thailand?
note: The Thai Baht is one of Asia's most stable currencies and has been used since 1897.

question: What currency is used in Malaysia?
note: Ringgit means 'jagged' in Malay, referring to the serrated edges of old Spanish silver coins.

question: What currency is used in Vietnam?
note: The Vietnamese Dong (VND) is one of the lowest-valued currencies — 1 USD ≈ 25,000 VND.

question: What currency is used in Argentina?
note: Argentina has had multiple currency crises — the Peso has been reissued several times.

question: What currency is used in Colombia?
note: The Colombian Peso (COP) has been in use since 1837.

question: What currency is used in Chile?
note: The Chilean Peso replaced the Escudo in 1975.

question: What currency is used in Peru?
note: The Peruvian Sol replaced the Inti in 1991. Sol means 'sun' in Spanish.

question: What currency is used in Philippines?
note: The Philippine Peso (PHP) was introduced in 1852 during Spanish colonial rule.

question: Which of these countries does NOT use the Euro?
note: Bulgaria uses the Lev (BGN). It is an EU member but has not yet joined the Eurozone.

question: Which two countries share the East Caribbean Dollar?
note: The East Caribbean Dollar is used by 8 island nations including Grenada, Antigua, and St Lucia.

question: What is the currency of Israel?
note: The Israeli New Shekel (ILS) replaced the old Shekel in 1986. Symbol: ₪.

question: What is the currency of Iran?
note: The Iranian Rial (IRR) is one of the world's lowest-valued currencies. Iranians informally use 'Toman' = 10 Rials.

question: What is the currency of Pakistan?
note: The Pakistani Rupee (PKR) has been used since the country's independence in 1947.

question: What is the currency of Bangladesh?
note: The Bangladeshi Taka replaced the Pakistani Rupee after independence in 1971.

question: What is the currency of Ghana?
note: The Ghanaian Cedi (GHS) — 'Cedi' comes from the Akan word for cowrie shell, once used as currency.

question: What is the currency of Ethiopia?
note: The Ethiopian Birr has been used since 1945. 'Birr' means 'silver' in Amharic.

question: What is the currency of Hungary?
note: Hungary uses the Forint (HUF) — it has chosen not to adopt the Euro despite being an EU member.

question: What is the currency of Czech Republic?
note: The Czech Koruna (CZK) replaced the Czechoslovak Koruna in 1993 after the country split.

question: What is the currency of New Zealand?
note: The New Zealand Dollar (NZD) replaced the Pound in 1967 when NZ decimalised.

question: Which country has a red circle on a white background?
note: Japan's Nisshōki — the red sun disc — has been in use since the 7th century.

question: Which country's flag has a maple leaf?
note: Canada's red and white flag with red maple leaf was adopted on 15 February 1965.

question: What are the three colours of the Italian flag?
note: Italy's vertical tricolour — green, white, red — was adopted in 1797.

question: What colour is the cross on Finland's flag?
note: Finland's flag shows a blue Nordic cross on white background — adopted in 1918.

question: Which country has a flag with a cedar tree?
note: Lebanon's flag features the Cedar of Lebanon, a symbol of immortality and steadiness.

question: What colour are the stars on the Brazilian flag?
note: The 27 white stars on Brazil's blue circle represent the 26 states and the Federal District.

question: Which is the only national flag that is not rectangular?
note: Nepal's flag consists of two stacked pennants — the only non-rectangular national flag.

question: Which country has a flag that is a perfect square?
note: Switzerland's flag is a perfect square — one of only two square national flags (Vatican is the other).

question: What colour is the background of the Japanese flag?
note: Japan's flag (Hinomaru) is a red circle on a white background.

question: Which country's flag features the Union Jack in the corner AND stars?
note: New Zealand's flag has the Union Jack plus four red stars of the Southern Cross on blue.

question: What are the Pan-African colours used on many African flags?
note: Red, Black, and Green were chosen by Marcus Garvey's Pan-African movement in 1920.

question: Which country's flag is all red with a yellow star?
note: Vietnam's flag — a yellow star on red — has been used since 1955.

question: What animal appears on the Mexican flag?
note: Mexico's coat of arms shows an eagle devouring a serpent on a cactus — from an Aztec legend.

question: Which Scandinavian country has a red flag with a white cross?
note: Denmark's Dannebrog — white cross on red — is one of the world's oldest national flags, dating to 1219.

question: What are the colours of the German flag?
note: Germany's tricolour — black, red, gold — was adopted by the Frankfurt Parliament in 1848.

question: Which country has a green flag with a white crescent and star?
note: Pakistan's flag — dark green with a white crescent and star — adopted in 1947 at independence.

question: What is written on Brazil's flag?
note: 'Ordem e Progresso' means Order and Progress in Portuguese — from Auguste Comte's positivism.

question: What colour is the background of the South Korean flag?
note: South Korea's Taegukgi has a white background with the Taiji symbol and four trigrams.

question: Which country's flag has the Sun of May?
note: Argentina's flag features the 'Sol de Mayo' — a sun with a human face — adopted in 1818.

question: What are the colours of the Dutch flag?
note: The Netherlands flag — red, white, blue — is one of the oldest tricolours, dating to 1572.

question: Which African country has a flag with a white star on blue and red?
note: Liberia's flag — stripes of red and white with a blue canton and white star — modelled on the US flag.

question: What colour is the cross on the Swedish flag?
note: Sweden's flag — a yellow/gold Nordic cross on blue — dates from at least the 16th century.

question: What animal is on Australia's coat of arms (also seen on official use)?
note: Australia's coat of arms features the kangaroo and emu — both cannot walk backwards, symbolising forward progress.

question: How many stripes are on the US flag?
note: The 13 stripes represent the original 13 colonies. The 50 stars represent the 50 states.

question: Which country has a flag featuring an AK-47 rifle?
note: Mozambique's flag features an AK-47 with a bayonet — the only national flag with a modern assault rifle.

question: What three colours appear on the Russian flag?
note: Russia's white-blue-red tricolour was introduced by Peter the Great in 1699, inspired by the Dutch flag.

question: Which country's flag has a red maple leaf?
note: Canada's maple leaf flag was adopted on 15 February 1965 after a national competition.

question: What is on the centre of the Saudi Arabian flag?
note: Saudi Arabia's flag bears the Shahada (Islamic creed) in white and a sword on green.

question: Which country has a dragon on its flag?
note: Bhutan's orange and yellow flag features the Druk (Thunder Dragon), giving Bhutan its local name 'Druk Yul'.

question: What colour is on Jamaica's flag in the X pattern?
note: Jamaica's flag has a gold/yellow diagonal cross (saltire) with black and green triangles.

question: Which country has a completely green flag with Arabic script?
note: Libya used a plain green flag from 1977 to 2011 under Gaddafi — the world's only single-colour national flag.

question: What do the five rings on the Olympic flag represent?
note: The five rings represent the five inhabited continents — Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, Oceania.

question: Which colour appears on BOTH the French and Dutch flags?
note: Both France (blue, white, red vertical) and Netherlands (red, white, blue horizontal) use the same three colours.

question: Which South American country has the same flag colours as Colombia and Venezuela?
note: Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela all use yellow, blue, and red — legacy of Gran Colombia.

question: What is the colour of the crescent and star on the Turkish flag?
note: Turkey's flag is a white crescent and star on a red background — adopted in 1844.

question: Which country's flag has the most colours?
note: Seychelles' flag has five coloured rays of blue, yellow, red, white, and green — the most colours in a national flag.

question: What animal features on New Zealand's coat of arms but NOT on its flag?
note: The kiwi appears on NZ's coat of arms. The flag has the Southern Cross stars and the Union Jack.

question: Which flag has a red stripe at top, white in middle, and black at bottom?
note: Germany's flag — black, red, gold — not red/white/black. Kuwait has green, white, red, black. Egypt: red, white, black.

question: What appears in the centre of India's national flag?
note: India's flag has a navy blue Ashoka Chakra (wheel with 24 spokes) on a white band between saffron and green.

question: Which European country has red, white, and blue colours arranged horizontally?
note: The Netherlands flag is horizontal red-white-blue. France is vertical. Luxembourg looks similar but lighter blue.

question: What is unique about the Swiss and Vatican flags compared to all other national flags?
note: Switzerland and Vatican City are the only countries with square national flags.

question: Which Nordic country's flag does NOT have a cross?
note: All five Nordic countries (Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden) have the Nordic cross on their flags.

question: What colour is the background of Pakistan's flag?
note: Pakistan's flag is predominantly dark green with a white vertical stripe on the left.

question: Which country has a flag with three equal horizontal bands of black, red, and gold?
note: Germany's federal flag is black (top), red (middle), gold/yellow (bottom).

question: What is the background colour of China's national flag?
note: China's flag is red with one large yellow star and four smaller yellow stars.

question: Which country's flag features a bald eagle?
note: The bald eagle appears on the US Presidential seal and coat of arms, though not on the flag itself.

question: What appears on the flag of Cambodia?
note: Cambodia's flag features Angkor Wat — making it one of the few flags to depict a building.

question: How many stars are on the Chinese national flag?
note: China's flag has 5 stars — one large star and four smaller ones, representing unity of the Chinese people.

question: Which Central American country has a blue and white flag with a coat of arms?
note: Honduras has a white horizontal stripe between two blue stripes, with five blue stars in the centre.

question: Which country's flag is divided diagonally into black and red halves with a red star?
note: Papua New Guinea's flag — black (top left) with yellow bird of paradise, red (bottom right) with Southern Cross stars.

question: Which country does NOT border France?
note: Portugal only borders Spain. France borders Belgium, Luxembourg, Germany, Switzerland, Italy, Monaco, Spain, and Andorra.

question: Which country borders BOTH China and India?
note: Nepal sits between China (Tibet) to the north and India to the south and west.

question: How many countries does Germany border?
note: Germany borders Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, France, Switzerland, Austria, Czech Republic, and Poland.

question: Which country is completely surrounded by South Africa?
note: Lesotho (officially the Kingdom of Lesotho) is a landlocked country entirely enclosed within South Africa.

question: Which country borders both Norway AND Russia?
note: Finland shares borders with Norway, Sweden, and Russia — making it geopolitically significant.

question: Which ocean lies between Europe and North America?
note: The Atlantic Ocean was named after the Titan Atlas from Greek mythology.

question: Which continent is Egypt on?
note: Egypt is in northeastern Africa, though the Sinai Peninsula touches Asia.

question: Which country shares the longest land border with the United States?
note: The US-Canada border is the world's longest international border at 8,891 km.

question: Which sea lies between Italy and Croatia?
note: The Adriatic Sea separates the Italian Peninsula from the Balkans.

question: Which continent has the most countries?
note: Africa has 54 recognized countries — the most of any continent.

question: What is the world's largest country by land area?
note: Russia covers 17.1 million km² — nearly twice the size of second-place Canada.

question: What is the world's smallest country by area?
note: Vatican City covers just 0.44 km² — smaller than many city parks.

question: Which is the world's longest river?
note: The Nile runs approximately 6,650 km through northeastern Africa to the Mediterranean.

question: On which continent is the Amazon rainforest?
note: The Amazon rainforest covers 5.5 million km² across 9 South American countries.

question: Which country has the most time zones?
note: France spans 12 time zones due to its overseas territories, more than any other country.

question: Which mountain range separates Europe from Asia?
note: The Ural Mountains form a natural boundary between European and Asian Russia.

question: What is the world's largest desert?
note: Antarctica is the world's largest desert at 14 million km². The Sahara is the largest hot desert.

question: Which country has the most land borders with other countries?
note: China and Russia are tied with 14 land borders each — the most of any country.

question: What is the deepest lake in the world?
note: Lake Baikal in Russia is 1,642m deep and contains 20% of the world's fresh surface water.

question: Which country is both in Europe and Asia?
note: Turkey (and Russia, Kazakhstan, Azerbaijan) span both continents. Turkey's Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia.

question: What is the tallest mountain in Africa?
note: Kilimanjaro in Tanzania is 5,895m — Africa's highest peak and the world's highest free-standing mountain.

question: Which country does the Amazon River flow through before reaching the sea?
note: The Amazon flows mainly through Brazil before emptying into the Atlantic Ocean near Marajó island.

question: What is the name of the sea between Saudi Arabia and Africa?
note: The Red Sea connects to the Mediterranean via the Suez Canal — one of the world's busiest shipping lanes.

question: Which is the only country to border both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans?
note: Colombia is the only South American country with coastlines on both the Pacific and Atlantic (Caribbean) oceans.

question: How many countries does the Amazon River pass through?
note: The Amazon passes through Peru, Colombia, and Brazil — 3 countries — before reaching the Atlantic.

question: Which African country has coastline on both the Atlantic and Indian Oceans?
note: South Africa's Cape of Good Hope is where the Atlantic and Indian Oceans officially meet.

question: What is the name of the strait between Spain and Morocco?
note: The Strait of Gibraltar is only 14 km wide at its narrowest point.

question: Which country is entirely surrounded by Italy?
note: San Marino is a landlocked microstate entirely surrounded by Italy — the world's oldest republic.

question: Which two continents are connected by the Isthmus of Panama?
note: The Isthmus of Panama joins North and South America. The Panama Canal cuts through it.

question: What is the largest island in the world?
note: Greenland is 2.16 million km² — though it's an autonomous territory of Denmark.

question: Which country contains most of the Sahara Desert?
note: Algeria contains the largest portion of the Sahara Desert at about 2 million km².

question: What is the highest peak in Europe (excluding Russia's Caucasus)?
note: Mont Blanc (4,808m) on the France-Italy border is Western Europe's highest peak.

question: Which sea does the Danube River flow into?
note: The Danube flows through 10 countries before emptying into the Black Sea via a delta in Romania.

question: What is the name of the channel between England and France?
note: The English Channel (La Manche in French) is 34 km at its narrowest at the Strait of Dover.

question: Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
note: Canada has a coastline of 202,080 km — the world's longest, including islands.

question: In which country is the Atacama Desert?
note: The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest non-polar desert on Earth.

question: What is the largest lake in Africa?
note: Lake Victoria is Africa's largest lake at 68,870 km² and the world's largest tropical lake.

question: Which country owns Easter Island (Rapa Nui)?
note: Easter Island is a Chilean territory in the Pacific Ocean, about 3,700 km off the Chilean coast.

question: Which European country has no land borders?
note: Iceland, Malta, and Cyprus are all island nations with no land borders.

question: What is the name of the large peninsula in southern Europe containing Spain and Portugal?
note: The Iberian Peninsula is separated from France by the Pyrenees mountains.

question: Which river runs through Egypt?
note: The Nile has sustained Egyptian civilisation for over 5,000 years.

question: Which country does the Yangtze River flow through?
note: The Yangtze is Asia's longest river at 6,300 km, flowing entirely within China.

question: What is the world's largest ocean?
note: The Pacific Ocean covers about 165 million km² — more than all the world's land combined.

question: Which country contains Angel Falls, the world's highest waterfall?
note: Angel Falls in Venezuela drops 979m — nearly 20 times the height of Niagara Falls.

question: What is the lowest point on Earth's surface?
note: The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean reaches 11,034m below sea level — the deepest point on Earth.

question: Which country has the most volcanoes?
note: Indonesia has over 130 active volcanoes — more than any other country.

question: What is the longest mountain range in the world?
note: The Andes run 7,000 km along South America's western coast.

question: Which African country does the equator pass through?
note: The equator passes through Kenya, Uganda, Democratic Republic of Congo, and several other African countries.

question: What sea lies between Italy and Tunisia?
note: The Mediterranean Sea separates Europe from Africa — its name means 'Sea in the Middle of the Earth'.

question: How many countries are in South America?
note: South America has 12 countries: Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Paraguay, Peru, Suriname, Uruguay, Venezuela.

question: Which strait separates Asia from North America?
note: The Bering Strait is only 85 km wide. During the last Ice Age, it was a land bridge allowing human migration.

question: What is the name of the sea between Australia and New Zealand?
note: The Tasman Sea (named after Dutch explorer Abel Tasman) separates Australia from New Zealand.

question: Which is the only country in both North America and Europe?
note: Denmark (technically in Europe) controls Greenland (geographically in North America). Iceland is entirely in Europe.

question: What is the name of the peninsula where Finland is largely located?
note: The Scandinavian Peninsula includes Norway and Sweden. Finland is on the Fennoscandian Shield.

question: Which body of water connects the Black Sea to the Mediterranean?
note: The Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits, separated by the Sea of Marmara, connect the Black Sea to the Mediterranean.

question: Which country has both Atlantic and Indian Ocean coastline on the same continent?
note: South Africa is at the southern tip of Africa where the two oceans meet at Cape Agulhas.

question: What is the largest country in Africa by area?
note: Algeria covers 2.38 million km² — the largest country in Africa and the Arab world.

question: Which river forms the boundary between the USA and Mexico?
note: The Rio Grande (or Río Bravo del Norte) forms about 2,020 km of the US-Mexico border.

question: In which ocean is the island of Madagascar?
note: Madagascar lies in the Indian Ocean, about 400 km off the east coast of Mozambique.

question: Which European country is split between continental Europe and an island?
note: Denmark's mainland is the Jutland Peninsula, plus about 400 islands including Zealand and Funen.

question: How many official languages does Switzerland have?
note: German, French, Italian, and Romansh — Switzerland is famously multilingual.

question: What is the official language of Brazil?
note: Brazil is the only Portuguese-speaking country in South America, legacy of Portuguese colonisation from 1500.

question: Which country has the most official languages?
note: South Africa has 11 official languages including Zulu, Xhosa, Afrikaans, and English.

question: What language is spoken by the most people as a first language?
note: Mandarin Chinese is spoken by about 920 million native speakers.

question: How many official languages does Belgium have?
note: Belgium has three: Dutch (Flemish), French, and German.

question: What is the official language of Egypt?
note: Modern Standard Arabic is Egypt's official language. Egyptian Arabic is the spoken dialect.

question: In which country is Swahili a major official language?
note: Swahili (Kiswahili) is co-official with English in Tanzania and Kenya, and widely spoken across East Africa.

question: Which language has the most native speakers in South America?
note: Spanish is the most spoken language in South America by native speakers.

question: How many official languages does the European Union have?
note: The EU has 24 official languages, with German being the most widely spoken native language.

question: What language do people in the Netherlands speak?
note: Dutch is spoken by about 23 million people worldwide, including in the Netherlands and Flanders, Belgium.

question: What is the most spoken language in Africa?
note: Arabic is spoken across North Africa and is the continent's most widely spoken language.

question: Which country speaks Farsi (Persian) as its official language?
note: Farsi is Iran's official language. Dari (Afghan Persian) is spoken in Afghanistan.

question: What is the official language of the Philippines alongside Filipino?
note: The Philippines has two official languages: Filipino (based on Tagalog) and English.

question: In which country is Catalan spoken?
note: Catalan is spoken in Catalonia, Valencian Community, and the Balearic Islands in Spain, plus Andorra.

question: What language do Brazilians NOT speak as an official language?
note: Brazil's only official language is Portuguese. English is not official.

question: Which is the most widely spoken language in Canada?
note: English is Canada's most widely spoken language. French is co-official and dominant in Quebec.

question: What script is used to write Japanese?
note: Japanese uses three writing systems: Hiragana, Katakana (both syllabic), and Kanji (logographic).

question: Which African country uses French as an official language?
note: Senegal uses French as its official language — a legacy of French colonial rule.

question: What is the official language of Israel?
note: Hebrew was revived as a modern spoken language in the 19th century. Arabic is also official.

question: Which country has both French and Wolof as widely spoken languages?
note: Senegal's official language is French but Wolof is the most widely spoken local language.

question: In which country is Amharic the official language?
note: Amharic is Ethiopia's official working language, spoken by about 22 million people as a first language.

question: What language is spoken in most of the Caribbean islands with French influence?
note: Haitian Creole is spoken by virtually all of Haiti's 11 million people.

question: Which country has Malay, Chinese, Tamil, and English as official languages?
note: Singapore's four official languages reflect its multicultural population.

question: What language is spoken in Iceland?
note: Icelandic has barely changed in 1,000 years — modern Icelanders can read medieval Viking sagas.

question: Which is NOT an official language of Switzerland?
note: Austrian is not a language — Austria speaks German. Switzerland's four languages are German, French, Italian, Romansh.

question: What language do people in Pakistan primarily speak?
note: Urdu is Pakistan's national language, though Punjabi is the most widely spoken regional language.

question: Which European language is spoken in Mozambique?
note: Mozambique was a Portuguese colony until 1975 and Portuguese remains the official language.

question: How many people speak English as either a first or second language?
note: Approximately 1.5 billion people speak English — about 400 million as native speakers.

question: Which language has the most letters in its alphabet?
note: The Khmer (Cambodian) alphabet has 74 letters — the largest alphabet in the world.

question: What is the official language of the Maldives?
note: Dhivehi is the Maldivian language, written in Thaana script which reads right to left.

question: Which country has the international dialling code +1?
note: Both the USA and Canada share +1 — it's the North American Numbering Plan.

question: Which country has the dialling code +44?
note: The UK's +44 was assigned in 1960 when international dialling was introduced.

question: Which country has the dialling code +61?
note: Australia's +61. New Zealand is +64, South Africa is +27.

question: Which country has the dialling code +49?
note: Germany's +49. Austria is +43, Switzerland is +41, Netherlands is +31.

question: Which country has the dialling code +81?
note: Japan's +81 was assigned in 1960.

question: Which country has the dialling code +86?
note: China's +86 is one of the world's most called codes due to its population size.

question: Which country has the dialling code +7?
note: Russia uses +7. Kazakhstan also uses +7 — the only two countries sharing this code.

question: Which country has the dialling code +33?
note: France's +33. Belgium is +32, Spain is +34, Switzerland is +41.

question: Which country has the dialling code +91?
note: India's +91. Pakistan is +92, Bangladesh is +880, Sri Lanka is +94.

question: Which country has the dialling code +55?
note: Brazil's +55. Argentina is +54, Chile is +56, Colombia is +57.

question: Which country has the dialling code +34?
note: Spain's +34. Portugal is +351, France is +33, Italy is +39.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .jp?
note: Japan's .jp has been registered since 1986.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .de?
note: Germany's .de (Deutschland) is the world's most used country code TLD.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .fi?
note: Finland's .fi domain has been registered since 1986.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .au?
note: Australia's .au. Austria uses .at, Azerbaijan uses .az.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .br?
note: Brazil's .br is one of the most populated country domains.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .cn?
note: China's .cn is the world's largest country code domain by registrations.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .se?
note: Sweden's .se. Switzerland uses .ch (from Confederation Helvetica).

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .za?
note: .za comes from Suid-Afrika, the Afrikaans name for South Africa.

question: What does the TLD .io originally stand for?
note: .io is the country code for British Indian Ocean Territory, now popular with tech companies.

question: Which country has the dialling code +354?
note: Iceland's +354 — one of the trickiest dial codes to remember.

question: Which country has the dialling code +358?
note: Finland's +358. Sweden is +46, Norway is +47, Estonia is +372.

question: Which country has the dialling code +82?
note: South Korea's +82. Japan is +81, China is +86.

question: Which country has the dialling code +52?
note: Mexico's +52. Colombia is +57, Argentina is +54, Chile is +56.

question: Which country has the dialling code +20?
note: Egypt's +20 was one of the earliest codes assigned, in 1960.

question: Which country uses the internet TLD .nl?
note: The Netherlands' .nl. Norway uses .no, New Zealand uses .nz, Nepal uses .np.

question: What country code does Amazon's .amazon TLD relate to?
answers: No country — it's a brand TLD
note: .amazon is a generic top-level domain (gTLD) owned by Amazon — not a country code.

question: Which country has the dialling code +27?
note: South Africa's +27. Zimbabwe is +263, Zambia is +260, Kenya is +254.

question: Which country has the dialling code +64?
note: New Zealand's +64. Australia is +61, Fiji is +679, Papua New Guinea is +675.

question: Which Caribbean island uses the TLD .cu?
note: Cuba's .cu has been registered since 1992.

question: What is the national animal of Australia?
note: The red kangaroo appears on Australia's coat of arms alongside the emu.

question: What is the national animal of Canada?
note: The Canadian beaver has been an official emblem since 1975.

question: What is the national animal of India?
note: The Bengal Tiger became India's national animal in 1973 under Project Tiger.

question: What is the national animal of New Zealand?
note: The kiwi (Apteryx) is a flightless nocturnal bird found only in New Zealand.

question: What is the national animal of Scotland?
note: The unicorn has been Scotland's national animal since the 12th century.

question: What is the national bird of the United States?
note: The bald eagle was chosen as the US national bird in 1782.

question: Which country is Sushi originally from?
note: Sushi originated in Japan, evolving from a method of preserving fish in fermented rice.

question: Which country is Paella originally from?
note: Paella originated in Valencia, Spain, in the 19th century.

question: Which country is Kimchi originally from?
note: Kimchi has been made in Korea for thousands of years and is a UNESCO Cultural Heritage.

question: Which country is Feta cheese traditionally from?
note: Feta is a PDO (Protected Designation of Origin) product — only made in specific Greek regions.

question: Injera flatbread is the national dish of which country?
note: Injera is made from teff grain and serves as both bread and eating utensil in Ethiopia.

question: Which country is Croissant originally from?
note: The croissant was invented in Vienna, Austria, and popularised in France in the early 19th century.

question: Which country is Chocolate originally from?
note: Cacao was first cultivated by Mesoamerican civilisations, including the Aztecs in what is now Mexico.

question: Which country did Pizza originate from?
note: Pizza originated in Naples, Italy, in the 18th-19th century.

question: Which country is famous for inventing Cheese Fondue?
note: Fondue was declared Switzerland's national dish in the 1930s as a way to increase cheese sales.

question: What is the national animal of China?
note: The giant panda has been a symbol of China and is used in conservation diplomacy ('panda diplomacy').

question: Which country is Haggis traditionally from?
note: Haggis — a savoury pudding made from sheep offal mixed with oatmeal — is Scotland's national dish.

question: In which country did the Carnival of Rio de Janeiro originate?
note: Rio Carnival is the world's largest carnival, attracting 2 million people per day.

question: What is the national drink of Japan?
note: Sake (rice wine) has been brewed in Japan for at least 2,000 years.

question: Which country is the world's largest producer of coffee?
note: Brazil produces about 35% of the world's coffee supply.

question: What sport was invented in Canada?
note: Basketball was invented in 1891 by Canadian James Naismith while teaching in Springfield, Massachusetts.

question: Which country is credited with inventing Football (Soccer)?
note: Modern association football was codified in England in 1863 by the Football Association.

question: What is the national dish of the United Kingdom?
note: Chicken Tikka Masala was declared Britain's national dish in 2001 by Foreign Secretary Robin Cook.

question: What is Flamenco?
note: Flamenco is a traditional art form from Andalusia, Spain, involving dance, guitar, and singing.

question: In which country is the Oktoberfest beer festival held annually?
note: Oktoberfest is held in Munich, Bavaria, Germany, and began in 1810.

question: What national animal appears on Mexico's flag?
note: Mexico's flag shows an eagle with a serpent in its beak, perched on a cactus — from an Aztec legend.

question: Which country is the world's largest producer of tea?
note: China produces about 45% of the world's tea.

question: Tango music and dance originated in which country?
note: Tango originated in the working-class areas of Buenos Aires in the late 19th century.

question: Which country celebrates Diwali as a major national holiday?
note: Diwali is the Hindu Festival of Lights — India's most widely celebrated festival.

question: What is the national flower of Japan?
note: Cherry blossom (sakura) season is celebrated annually across Japan in late March/April.

question: Which country's cuisine commonly uses injera as a staple?
note: Injera is used across Ethiopia and Eritrea as a staple — it's both the plate and the utensil.

question: In which country is the running of the bulls (San Fermín) held?
note: The San Fermín festival and running of the bulls is held in Pamplona, Spain, every July.

question: Which country is home to the ancient ruins of Machu Picchu?
note: Machu Picchu was built by the Inca Empire in the 15th century at 2,430m altitude in Peru.

question: What is the national animal of Norway?
note: The moose (elk) is Norway's national animal — there are about 120,000 in Norway.

question: Which Asian country is famous for its Muay Thai martial art?
note: Muay Thai (Thai boxing) is Thailand's national sport and martial art, using fists, elbows, knees, and shins.

question: What is the national animal of Finland?
note: The brown bear has been Finland's national animal since 1985.

question: Jollof Rice is associated with which region?
note: Jollof rice is a beloved West African dish — Nigeria and Ghana famously debate who makes it best.

question: Which country invented the printing press?
note: Johannes Gutenberg invented the printing press with movable type in Mainz, Germany, around 1440.

question: What is the traditional Japanese art of paper folding called?
note: Origami has been practised in Japan since the 6th century when paper was introduced from China.

question: Which country is famous for its Mariachi music?
note: Mariachi is a form of regional Mexican music from Jalisco, now a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.

question: In which country is the Holi festival of colours most prominently celebrated?
note: Holi is a Hindu spring festival celebrated across India and Nepal with coloured powder and water.

question: Which country is credited with inventing the telephone?
note: Alexander Graham Bell (Scottish-born, working in Canada and USA) patented the telephone in 1876.

question: What is the national sport of Canada?
note: Canada officially recognises both ice hockey (winter) and lacrosse (summer) as national sports.

question: Which country's national animal is the snow leopard?
note: The snow leopard (Panthera uncia) is Pakistan's national animal.

question: In which country did the martial art Judo originate?
note: Judo was created in 1882 by Japanese educator Jigorō Kanō as a sport derived from jujutsu.

question: What is Capoeira?
note: Capoeira is an Afro-Brazilian martial art combining elements of dance, acrobatics, and music, created by enslaved Africans in Brazil.

question: Which country is the world's largest producer of olive oil?
note: Spain produces about 45% of the world's olive oil, mainly in Andalusia.

question: What is the national animal of Russia?
note: The bear (specifically the brown bear) is Russia's national symbol and cultural emblem.

question: In which country did the Renaissance begin?
note: The Renaissance began in Florence, Italy, in the 14th century, spreading across Europe.

question: Which country has the world's largest Buddhist temple complex?
note: Borobudur in Indonesia is the world's largest Buddhist temple, built in the 9th century.

question: What is the world's largest economy by GDP?
note: The US GDP is about $27 trillion — the world's largest, though China may overtake it by 2030.

question: Which country has the highest GDP per capita?
note: Luxembourg's GDP per capita is around $130,000 — the world's highest.

question: What is the dominant export of Saudi Arabia?
note: Oil accounts for about 70% of Saudi Arabia's export earnings and 40% of GDP.

question: Which country is the world's largest exporter of goods?
note: China became the world's largest goods exporter in 2009 and maintains that position.

question: What is Germany's largest company by market capitalisation?
note: SAP (software company) is Germany's most valuable company, overtaking BMW and Siemens.

question: Which country has the world's largest stock exchange by market cap?
note: The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) is the world's largest stock exchange.

question: Which country produces the most cars annually?
note: China has been the world's largest car manufacturer since 2008.

question: What is Switzerland's most famous export industry?
note: Swiss banking and financial services are world-renowned, alongside watches, chemicals, and machinery.

question: Which country is the world's largest wheat exporter?
note: Russia overtook the USA as the world's largest wheat exporter around 2016.

question: What is the GDP of the entire African continent roughly equal to?
note: Africa's combined GDP is roughly equal to Germany's — despite having 1.4 billion people.

question: Which country is the world's largest diamond producer?
note: Russia is the world's largest diamond producer by volume through Alrosa mining company.

question: Which country has the world's largest proven oil reserves?
note: Venezuela has the world's largest proven oil reserves at 303 billion barrels.

question: What is Japan's most famous car brand?
note: Toyota is the world's largest car manufacturer by sales and Japan's most valuable brand.

question: In which country is IKEA originally from?
note: IKEA was founded in 1943 by Ingvar Kamprad in Älmhult, Sweden.

question: Which country is H&M (clothing chain) originally from?
note: H&M (Hennes & Mauritz) was founded in 1947 in Västerås, Sweden.

question: Which country is home to the pharmaceutical company Novartis?
note: Novartis is headquartered in Basel, Switzerland — one of the world's largest pharma companies.

question: In which country is Toyota headquartered?
note: Toyota is headquartered in Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture, Japan.

question: Which country is home to Samsung?
note: Samsung is headquartered in Suwon, South Korea. It accounts for about 20% of South Korea's exports.

question: Which country has the world's largest fishing industry by catch?
note: China accounts for about 35% of global fish catch.

question: What is Norway's primary source of government revenue?
note: Norway's oil and gas sector accounts for about 14% of GDP and funds its $1.7 trillion sovereign wealth fund.

question: Which country is the world's largest cocoa producer?
note: Ivory Coast (Côte d'Ivoire) produces about 45% of the world's cocoa.

question: Which country is the leading producer of lithium?
note: Australia is the world's largest lithium producer, accounting for about 55% of global supply.

question: In which country is the Airbus company headquartered?
note: Airbus SE is headquartered in Toulouse, France, though it has major facilities across Europe.

question: Which country is the world's largest exporter of bananas?
note: Ecuador is the world's largest banana exporter, accounting for about 25% of global exports.

question: Which nation has the world's highest minimum wage (in USD equivalent)?
note: Switzerland's minimum wage in Geneva is over $25/hour — the world's highest.

question: In which country is Zara (clothing) originally from?
note: Zara was founded by Amancio Ortega in Arteixo, Galicia, Spain in 1975.

question: Which country is the world's largest producer of rubber?
note: Thailand produces about 37% of the world's natural rubber.

question: What is Finland's most famous technology company?
note: Nokia was once the world's largest mobile phone manufacturer, peaking in the early 2000s.

question: Which country produces the most wool globally?
note: Australia produces about 25% of the world's wool and is the largest supplier of merino wool.

question: Which country has the most billionaires?
note: The USA has the most billionaires (over 700) followed by China.

question: Which country is the world's largest exporter of soybeans?
note: Brazil overtook the USA as the world's largest soybean exporter around 2013.

question: In which country is the world's largest sovereign wealth fund?
note: Norway's Government Pension Fund Global is the world's largest at over $1.7 trillion.

question: Which country produces the most electricity from nuclear power?
note: The USA generates the most nuclear power in absolute terms — France generates the highest percentage (about 70%).

question: Which country has the world's largest economy by purchasing power parity (PPP)?
note: China surpassed the USA in GDP (PPP) around 2017 according to World Bank estimates.

question: In which country is the cosmetics brand L'Oréal from?
note: L'Oréal was founded in 1909 by Eugène Schueller in Paris, France.

question: Which is the world's most visited tourist destination country?
note: France is the world's most visited country, receiving about 90 million tourists per year.

question: What is the most traded currency pair in the world?
note: EUR/USD accounts for about 20-28% of all daily forex trading volume.

question: Which country is the world's largest exporter of arms?
note: The USA accounts for about 40% of global arms exports.

question: What percentage of the world's internet users are in Asia?
note: Over 55% of the world's internet users are in Asia, primarily China and India.

question: In which country is the chocolate brand Toblerone from?
note: Toblerone was created in 1908 in Bern, Switzerland — its distinctive triangular shape represents the Alps.

question: Which country spans the most time zones?
note: France spans 12 time zones due to overseas territories — more than Russia's 11 contiguous ones.

question: Three of these use the Euro. Which does NOT?
note: Serbia uses the Dinar. Slovakia (2009), Slovenia (2007), Croatia (2023) joined the Eurozone.

question: Which country is completely surrounded by only one other country?
note: Lesotho is surrounded entirely by South Africa. Monaco, Vatican City, and San Marino are surrounded by one country but on coast or near border.

question: What is the only country with a coastline on both the Red Sea and the Arabian Sea?
note: Yemen has coastlines on both the Red Sea (west) and the Arabian Sea/Gulf of Aden (south).

question: Which country has the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites?
note: Italy leads with 58 UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

question: What is the only Central American country without a Pacific Ocean coastline?
note: Belize only has a Caribbean/Atlantic coastline — no Pacific coast.

question: Which country has the world's longest written constitution?
note: India's constitution is the world's longest, with 448 articles in its current form.

question: Which country did NOT exist as an independent state before 1990?
note: Belarus was part of the Soviet Union until 1991. Estonia and Slovenia also gained independence around that time, but Portugal has been independent since 1143.

question: What is the world's oldest surviving republic?
note: San Marino claims to be the world's oldest republic, founded in 301 AD.

question: Which European country has the highest proportion of forest cover?
note: Finland has about 73% forest cover — the highest proportion in Europe.

question: How many countries are in the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS)?
note: The CIS has 9 full members: Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, and Moldova.

question: Which country has the highest altitude capital city in the world?
note: Sucre and La Paz (Bolivia) compete for this title. La Paz (seat of government) is at 3,640m, making it the highest by most measures.

question: What is the only country that borders both the Caspian Sea and the Persian Gulf?
note: Iran has coastlines on both the Caspian Sea (north) and Persian Gulf (south).

question: Which country has the most islands?
note: Sweden has about 221,800 islands — the most of any country. (Indonesia has the most inhabited islands.)

question: What is the world's newest internationally recognised country?
note: South Sudan gained independence from Sudan on 9 July 2011, making it the world's newest country.

question: Which country has the world's longest railway network?
note: The USA has the world's longest railway network at about 293,564 km.

question: Which country produces 90% of the world's saffron?
note: Iran produces about 90% of the world's saffron — the world's most expensive spice by weight.

question: What is the only country to have won the FIFA World Cup on a different continent?
note: Brazil won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden — the only country to win on a continent other than their own.

question: Which is the only country that begins with 'The'?
note: The Bahamas and The Gambia are officially named with 'The' — the only two countries in the world.

question: What percentage of the world's population lives in China and India combined?
note: China (~1.4B) and India (~1.45B) together account for about 36% of the world's population.

question: Which African country was never colonised by a European power?
note: Ethiopia successfully resisted Italian colonisation at the Battle of Adwa in 1896. Liberia is another example.

question: What is the most common religion in Indonesia?
note: Indonesia is the world's largest Muslim-majority country with about 87% Muslim population.

question: Which country has the world's largest army by active personnel?
note: China's People's Liberation Army has about 2 million active personnel — the world's largest.

question: Which country has the highest population density in the world?
note: Monaco has about 26,150 people per km² — the world's highest population density.

question: What is the world's most spoken language by total speakers (native + second language)?
note: English has approximately 1.5 billion total speakers, making it the most spoken globally.

question: Which country has the world's highest average elevation?
note: If Tibet were a country, it would win easily. Among recognised countries, Bhutan has the highest average elevation at about 3,280m.

question: What is the world's busiest airport by passenger numbers?
note: Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport has been the world's busiest since 1998 (except during COVID).

question: Which two countries share the world's highest navigable lake?
note: Lake Titicaca at 3,812m altitude is shared between Bolivia and Peru.

question: Which is the only Arab country to have hosted the FIFA World Cup?
note: Qatar hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup — the first time in the Arab world.

question: What is the only country to have a coastline on three oceans?
note: Russia has coastlines on the Arctic Ocean, Pacific Ocean, and Atlantic Ocean (via Baltic/Black Seas).

question: Which country has the most languages spoken within its borders?
note: Papua New Guinea has over 800 languages — the most linguistic diversity of any country.

question: Which European country has the lowest population density?
note: Iceland has about 3.6 people per km² — the lowest in Europe.

question: What is unique about Nauru as a country?
note: Nauru has no official capital — it's the world's only country without a capital city.

question: Which country is the world's largest per-capita carbon emitter?
note: Qatar emits about 35 tonnes of CO₂ per person per year — the world's highest per capita.

question: How many countries have French as an official language?
note: French is an official language in 29 countries — more countries than any language except English.

question: Which country has the highest rate of internet usage?
note: Iceland has near 100% internet penetration — the highest in the world.

question: What is the only country in the Americas where the majority religion is not Christianity?
note: Suriname has a plurality Hindu population (~27%) with significant Muslim (~20%) and Christian communities.

question: Which country has the world's largest methane reserves?
note: Russia has the world's largest natural gas (methane) reserves at about 38 trillion cubic metres.

question: How many countries are in the United Nations?
note: The UN has 193 member states as of 2011 (when South Sudan joined).

question: Which is the world's least visited country?
note: Nauru receives fewer than 200 tourists per year — the world's least visited country.

question: Which country has the most UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage items?
note: China has the most UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage inscriptions.

question: What is the smallest country in Asia by area?
note: The Maldives is the smallest Asian country at 298 km² and also the lowest-lying country in the world.

question: Which country has the most pyramids in the world?
note: Sudan has between 200-255 pyramids — more than Egypt, though they are smaller.

question: Which country was the first to give women the right to vote?
note: New Zealand became the first self-governing country to grant women the right to vote, in 1893.

question: What is the deepest point in the Mediterranean Sea?
note: The Calypso Deep in the Ionian Sea reaches 5,267m — the deepest point in the Mediterranean.

question: Which country has the highest percentage of renewable energy in its electricity mix?
note: Iceland generates about 100% of its electricity from renewables (geothermal and hydro).

question: What is the only country whose name in English ends in a vowel 'a' after an island?
note: Jamaica ends with 'a' and is an island. Many countries end in 'a' — the island qualifier is the trick.

question: Which country has the world's oldest national anthem?
note: The Wilhelmus (Netherlands) dates from around 1568-1572 — the world's oldest national anthem.

question: Which country has the most natural lakes?
note: Canada has about 879,800 lakes — more than any other country, about 60% of the world's lakes.

question: What is the world's most biodiverse country?
note: Brazil is considered the world's most biodiverse country, home to 20% of all species on Earth.

question: Which country has the most volcanoes?
note: Indonesia has over 130 active volcanoes on the Pacific Ring of Fire.

question: What is the highest recorded temperature ever on Earth?
note: 56.7°C (134°F) was recorded at Furnace Creek, Death Valley, California on 10 July 1913.

question: Which country has the world's largest navy?
note: China surpassed the US Navy in total number of ships around 2020.

question: What percentage of Earth's fresh water is in Antarctica?
note: Antarctica holds approximately 70% of the world's fresh water in its ice sheets.

question: Which country has produced the most Nobel Prize winners per capita?
note: Sweden has won more Nobel Prizes per capita than any other country.

question: What is the only country that begins and ends with the same letter?
note: Armenia begins and ends with 'a'. So does Algeria, Albania, and several others — but Armenia is a common answer.

question: Which country has the most border crossings with other nations?
note: China has 14 land borders — tied with Russia for the most, but has more formal border crossings.

question: In which country is the world's longest road tunnel?
note: The Laerdal Tunnel in Norway is 24.5 km long — the world's longest road tunnel, opened in 2000.

question: Which country has the world's tallest building?
note: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai, UAE, is 828m tall — the world's tallest building since 2010.

question: How long is the Great Wall of China?
note: All sections of the Great Wall total about 21,196 km according to a 2012 Chinese survey.

question: Which country is home to the world's largest coral reef?
note: The Great Barrier Reef off Queensland, Australia, is the world's largest coral reef system at 2,300 km.

question: What is the name of the world's largest hot desert?
note: The Sahara covers 9.2 million km² across North Africa — the world's largest hot desert.

question: Which country has the most medals at the Summer Olympics (all time)?
note: The USA leads the all-time Olympic medals table with over 2,600 total medals.

question: What fraction of the world's countries are in the southern hemisphere?
note: About one-third of the world's countries are primarily in the southern hemisphere.

question: Which country has the most coastline per km² of land area?
note: Norway has 83,281 km of coastline (including fjords) relative to its land area — the most per km² of land.

question: Which country has the world's highest homicide rate?
note: Honduras has had one of the world's highest homicide rates in recent years.

question: Which country has never been to war?
note: Liechtenstein has not been involved in a war since 1866 and actually disbanded its army after that.

question: How many countries are there in the ASEAN bloc?
note: ASEAN has 10 member states: Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam.

question: Which is the only country to have left the European Union?
note: The United Kingdom left the EU on 31 January 2020 — the only country ever to do so.

question: Which country has the world's most expensive real estate per square metre?
note: Monaco has the world's most expensive real estate at over $60,000 per m².

question: What is the world's oldest capital city?
note: Damascus has been continuously inhabited for over 10,000 years — widely considered the world's oldest capital.

question: Which country has the most non-resident Indian population?
note: The USA has the world's largest Indian diaspora, though UAE has the highest as a percentage of its population.

question: What is the name of the sea that borders Sweden, Finland, and the Baltic states?
note: The Baltic Sea is a brackish sea, bordered by Sweden, Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Germany, and Denmark.

question: Which country has the world's largest mangrove forest?
note: The Sundarbans in Bangladesh (and India) is the world's largest mangrove forest.

question: What is the world's most isolated capital city?
note: Wellington, New Zealand, is considered the world's most isolated capital from other major capitals.

question: Which country has the most active nuclear power plants?
note: The USA has 93 operational nuclear reactors — more than any other country.

question: How many countries make up the United Kingdom?
note: The UK is made up of 4 countries: England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.

question: Which is the least densely populated country in the world?
note: Mongolia has about 2 people per km² — the world's least densely populated sovereign nation.

question: Which country built the first commercial nuclear power plant?
note: The USSR built the world's first commercial nuclear power plant at Obninsk in 1954.

question: What is the official name of Libya?
answers: Great Socialist People's Libyan Arab Jamahiriya
note: Since 2011, Libya's official name has been 'State of Libya'.

question: Which country consumes the most coffee per capita?
note: Finland consumes about 12 kg of coffee per person per year — the highest in the world.

question: Which country has the world's highest rate of vegetarianism?
note: India has the world's highest vegetarianism rate — about 30-40% of the population.

question: Which body of water has the highest salt concentration?
note: The Dead Sea has about 34% salinity — about 10x saltier than the ocean.

question: Which country has the world's longest national anthem by duration?
note: Uruguay's national anthem is about 6 minutes long when performed in full — the world's longest.

question: Which country is home to the world's largest stadium?
note: The Rungrado 1st of May Stadium in Pyongyang, North Korea, holds 114,000 people.

question: What is the name of the geographic line dividing North and South Korea?
note: The Korean Demilitarised Zone (DMZ) runs roughly along the 38th Parallel North.

question: Which country is the world's largest producer of rubber?
note: Thailand produces about 37% of the world's natural rubber.

question: Which two countries share the world's largest freshwater lake?
note: Lake Superior, the world's largest freshwater lake by surface area, is shared by Canada and the USA.

question: How many countries does the Tropic of Cancer pass through?
note: The Tropic of Cancer passes through 16 countries including Mexico, India, China, and several in Africa.

question: Which country has the most World Heritage Sites in Asia?
note: China has 57 UNESCO World Heritage Sites — the most in Asia.

question: What is the world's most visited national park?
note: Great Smoky Mountains National Park in Tennessee/North Carolina receives about 12 million visitors annually.

question: Which country has the highest percentage of women in parliament?
note: Rwanda leads the world with about 61% women in parliament.

question: What is the deepest lake in Africa?
note: Lake Tanganyika is 1,470m deep — the second deepest lake in the world after Baikal.

question: Which is the smallest country in the Americas?
note: Saint Kitts and Nevis covers just 261 km² — the smallest country in the Americas.

question: Which country has the highest average IQ according to global studies?
note: Japan consistently scores highest in international studies, alongside Singapore and South Korea.

question: How many countries border the Mediterranean Sea?
note: 21 countries have Mediterranean coastlines across Southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.

question: Which country was formerly known as Persia?
note: Iran was known as Persia to the Western world until 1935 when Reza Shah asked nations to use 'Iran'.

question: What is the world's largest river by volume of water discharged?
note: The Amazon discharges about 20% of all river water that flows into the world's oceans.

question: Which country has the world's highest average age at death?
note: Japan has the world's highest life expectancy and consistently leads in longevity statistics.

question: Which country has the most land borders it shares with others — tied for first place with China?
note: Russia and China both share land borders with 14 countries — more than any other nation.
